questions.question_text: Pressure is defined as the force acting per unit ________ of a surface.
questions.subject: science
questions.chapter: Pressure, Winds, Storms, and Cyclones
questions.answer_explanation: Pressure is the ratio of force to the area over which it is applied (P = F/A).

questions.question_text: Why do school bags often have wide straps instead of thin strings?
questions.subject: science
questions.chapter: Pressure, Winds, Storms, and Cyclones
questions.answer_explanation: A larger area (wide straps) reduces the pressure exerted by the weight of the bag on the shoulders, making it more comfortable to carry.

options.option_text: To decrease the pressure on shoulders
To make the bag look bigger

questions.question_text: Which of the following statements about air pressure is correct?
questions.subject: science
questions.chapter: Pressure, Winds, Storms, and Cyclones
questions.answer_explanation: Air particles move in all directions and collide with surfaces, exerting pressure equally in all directions.

options.option_text: Air exerts pressure in all directions
Air pressure is highest at the top of a mountain

questions.question_text: What happens to the pressure of air when its speed increases?
questions.subject: science
questions.chapter: Pressure, Winds, Storms, and Cyclones
questions.answer_explanation: According to Bernoulli's principle, an increase in the speed of a fluid (like air) is accompanied by a decrease in pressure.

questions.question_text: High-speed winds blowing over the roof of a hut can lift the roof because:
questions.subject: science
questions.chapter: Pressure, Winds, Storms, and Cyclones
questions.answer_explanation: Fast wind over the roof reduces pressure above it. The higher pressure inside the hut then pushes the roof upwards.

options.option_text: High speed increases air pressure on top
High speed creates low pressure on top
The wind pushes the roof down

questions.question_text: Air moves from a region of __________ pressure to a region of __________ pressure.
questions.subject: science
questions.chapter: Pressure, Winds, Storms, and Cyclones
questions.answer_explanation: The difference in air pressure causes air to flow from areas where it is more concentrated (high pressure) to where it is less concentrated (low pressure).

questions.question_text: On heating, air __________ and becomes __________.
questions.subject: science
questions.chapter: Pressure, Winds, Storms, and Cyclones
questions.answer_explanation: When air is heated, its particles move faster and spread out (expand), making it less dense and lighter than cold air.

questions.question_text: Which instrument is used to measure wind speed?
questions.subject: science
questions.chapter: Pressure, Winds, Storms, and Cyclones
questions.answer_explanation: An anemometer is a device used for measuring the speed and direction of the wind.

questions.question_text: A balloon expands when we blow air into it because:
questions.subject: science
questions.chapter: Pressure, Winds, Storms, and Cyclones
questions.answer_explanation: Adding more air particles increases collisions with the inner walls, creating internal pressure that stretches the balloon.

options.option_text: Air inside exerts pressure on the walls

questions.question_text: What is the primary cause of wind circulation on Earth?
questions.subject: science
questions.chapter: Pressure, Winds, Storms, and Cyclones
questions.answer_explanation: Uneven heating between the equator and poles, and between land and water, creates pressure differences that drive wind.

options.option_text: Uneven heating of the Earth by the Sun

questions.question_text: During the day in coastal areas, wind usually blows from:
questions.subject: science
questions.chapter: Pressure, Winds, Storms, and Cyclones
questions.answer_explanation: Land heats up faster than water; the hot air over land rises, creating low pressure that draws in cooler air from the sea (Sea Breeze).

questions.question_text: Which of the following is a characteristic of a thunderstorm?
questions.subject: science
questions.chapter: Pressure, Winds, Storms, and Cyclones
questions.answer_explanation: Thunderstorms develop in hot, humid tropical areas where rising warm air carries water droplets that freeze and fall, creating lightning and sound.

questions.question_text: The center of a cyclone is a calm area called the:
questions.subject: science
questions.chapter: Pressure, Winds, Storms, and Cyclones
questions.answer_explanation: The 'eye' of a cyclone is a low-pressure area with very light winds and clear skies, surrounded by high-speed rotating winds.

questions.question_text: Cyclones are known as __________ in the American continent.
questions.subject: science
questions.chapter: Pressure, Winds, Storms, and Cyclones
questions.answer_explanation: While they are the same weather phenomenon, they are called Hurricanes in the Atlantic and Typhoons in the Pacific.

questions.question_text: A dark funnel-shaped cloud that reaches from the sky to the ground is a:
questions.subject: science
questions.chapter: Pressure, Winds, Storms, and Cyclones
questions.answer_explanation: Tornadoes are violent, rotating columns of air that descend from thunderstorms to the ground.

questions.question_text: Why are holes made in banners and hoardings hanging in the open?
questions.subject: science
questions.chapter: Pressure, Winds, Storms, and Cyclones
questions.answer_explanation: Holes allow air to pass through the banner, reducing the force/pressure exerted by the wind so the banner doesn't tear or fall.

options.option_text: To reduce the air pressure exerted by the wind

questions.question_text: If you blow air between two hanging balloons, they will:
questions.subject: science
questions.chapter: Pressure, Winds, Storms, and Cyclones
questions.answer_explanation: Blowing air between them creates a region of low pressure; the higher pressure on the outside pushes the balloons toward each other.

questions.question_text: Thunder is caused by:
questions.subject: science
questions.chapter: Pressure, Winds, Storms, and Cyclones
questions.answer_explanation: Lightning heats the surrounding air so fast that it expands explosively, creating the sound wave we call thunder.

options.option_text: The rapid expansion of air heated by lightning

questions.question_text: During a thunderstorm, it is safest to take shelter in:
questions.subject: science
questions.chapter: Pressure, Winds, Storms, and Cyclones
questions.answer_explanation: Buildings and cars (acting as Faraday cages) protect you from lightning, whereas tall objects or open spaces increase risk.

options.option_text: Inside a car or a building
Under an umbrella with a metal handle

questions.question_text: The 'Standard Unit' (SI) of pressure is:
questions.subject: science
questions.chapter: Pressure, Winds, Storms, and Cyclones
questions.answer_explanation: One Pascal (Pa) is equal to one Newton per square meter (N/m²).

questions.question_text: What is the direction of wind during the 'Winter Monsoon' in India?
questions.subject: science
questions.chapter: Pressure, Winds, Storms, and Cyclones
questions.answer_explanation: In winter, land cools faster than the sea. High pressure over land pushes dry winds toward the low pressure over the sea.

questions.question_text: If a force of 100 N is applied to an area of 2 m², the pressure is:
questions.subject: science
questions.chapter: Pressure, Winds, Storms, and Cyclones
questions.answer_explanation: Pressure = Force / Area = 100 N / 2 m² = 50 Pa.

questions.question_text: What provides the energy for the formation of a cyclone?
questions.subject: science
questions.chapter: Pressure, Winds, Storms, and Cyclones
questions.answer_explanation: When water vapor changes back to liquid (rain), it releases heat into the atmosphere, which warms the air and fuels the cyclone's rotation.

options.option_text: Heat released when water vapor condenses into rain
The friction of wind on land

questions.question_text: Which coast of India is more vulnerable to cyclones?
questions.subject: science
questions.chapter: Pressure, Winds, Storms, and Cyclones
questions.answer_explanation: The East Coast (Bay of Bengal) is generally more prone to frequent and intense cyclones compared to the West Coast.

questions.question_text: A person standing in the 'Eye' of a cyclone would experience:
questions.subject: science
questions.chapter: Pressure, Winds, Storms, and Cyclones
questions.answer_explanation: The eye is a low-pressure region of sinking air, which suppresses cloud formation and wind, creating a brief 'calm before the storm' effect.

questions.question_text: Which of the following is the fundamental idea of the particulate nature of matter?
questions.subject: science
questions.chapter: Particulate Nature of Matter
questions.answer_explanation: Matter is not a continuous block; it is composed of very small particles with spaces between them.

options.option_text: Matter is continuous like a sheet of glass
Matter is made up of tiny, discrete particles
Matter is only made of air and water

questions.question_text: In which state of matter are the particles packed most closely together in a fixed, regular pattern?
questions.subject: science
questions.chapter: Particulate Nature of Matter
questions.answer_explanation: In solids, particles are closely packed with very strong forces of attraction, giving them a fixed shape.

questions.question_text: What happens to the movement of particles when the temperature of a substance increases?
questions.subject: science
questions.chapter: Particulate Nature of Matter
questions.answer_explanation: Increasing temperature adds energy to the particles, causing them to move or vibrate more rapidly.

questions.question_text: The smell of hot sizzling food reaches you several metres away, but to get the smell from cold food you have to go close. This is due to:
questions.subject: science
questions.chapter: Particulate Nature of Matter
questions.answer_explanation: Diffusion is the intermixing of particles. At higher temperatures, particles move faster and diffuse more quickly into the air.

questions.question_text: Which state of matter is highly compressible?
questions.subject: science
questions.chapter: Particulate Nature of Matter
questions.answer_explanation: Gases have large spaces between particles, allowing them to be easily pushed closer together under pressure.

questions.question_text: When a crystal of potassium permanganate is placed in water, the purple colour spreads throughout. This proves that:
questions.subject: science
questions.chapter: Particulate Nature of Matter
questions.answer_explanation: The spreading of color occurs because the particles of both the crystal and the water are in constant random motion.

options.option_text: Particles of matter are constantly moving

questions.question_text: The force of attraction between particles is weakest in:
questions.subject: science
questions.chapter: Particulate Nature of Matter
questions.answer_explanation: Oxygen is a gas. In gases, the forces of attraction between particles are negligible compared to liquids and solids.

questions.question_text: Which of the following describes the 'Liquid' state?
questions.subject: science
questions.chapter: Particulate Nature of Matter
questions.answer_explanation: Liquids take the shape of the container they are in, but their volume remains constant because particles stay close together.

options.option_text: No fixed shape but fixed volume
Fixed shape but no fixed volume
No fixed shape and no fixed volume

questions.question_text: What occupies the 'empty space' between particles in a gas?
questions.subject: science
questions.chapter: Particulate Nature of Matter
questions.answer_explanation: The space between the fundamental particles of a pure substance is empty (void/vacuum).

questions.question_text: Brownian motion describes:
questions.subject: science
questions.chapter: Particulate Nature of Matter
questions.answer_explanation: Brownian motion is the random, irregular movement of small particles (like pollen or dust) suspended in a fluid, caused by collisions with molecules.

options.option_text: The zig-zag random motion of particles

questions.question_text: If you compress a gas in a syringe, what decreases?
questions.subject: science
questions.chapter: Particulate Nature of Matter
questions.answer_explanation: Compression brings the particles closer together, thereby reducing the empty space between them.

questions.question_text: A balloon expands when you blow air into it because:
questions.subject: science
questions.chapter: Particulate Nature of Matter
questions.answer_explanation: Moving air particles collide with the inner walls of the balloon, exerting pressure that pushes the walls outward.

options.option_text: Air particles take up space and collide with the walls
The air turns into a solid

questions.question_text: Which of the following cannot flow?
questions.subject: science
questions.chapter: Particulate Nature of Matter
questions.answer_explanation: Wood is a solid. Particles in a solid are held in fixed positions and cannot slide over each other to flow.

questions.question_text: Two atoms of Hydrogen combine to form a stable particle called a:
questions.subject: science
questions.chapter: Particulate Nature of Matter
questions.answer_explanation: When atoms combine chemically, they form a molecule, which is the smallest unit of a substance that can exist independently.

questions.question_text: The interparticle distance is maximum in:
questions.subject: science
questions.chapter: Particulate Nature of Matter
questions.answer_explanation: Water vapor is the gaseous state of water, where particles are farthest apart.

questions.question_text: What happens when you dissolve sugar in water regarding the volume?
questions.subject: science
questions.chapter: Particulate Nature of Matter
questions.answer_explanation: Sugar particles fit into the small spaces between water particles, so the total volume does not increase noticeably.

questions.question_text: Particles in a __________ only vibrate about their fixed positions.
questions.subject: science
questions.chapter: Particulate Nature of Matter
questions.answer_explanation: In solids, the kinetic energy is low and forces are strong, so particles cannot move away from their fixed spots.

questions.question_text: Which of the following is NOT an example of matter?
questions.subject: science
questions.chapter: Particulate Nature of Matter
questions.answer_explanation: Matter must have mass and occupy space. A feeling is a sensation/emotion and does not consist of particles.

questions.question_text: When we pour a liquid from a flask into a bowl, it changes its:
questions.subject: science
questions.chapter: Particulate Nature of Matter
questions.answer_explanation: Liquids have a fixed volume but no fixed shape; they take the shape of the container.

questions.question_text: The density of matter is generally highest in which state?
questions.subject: science
questions.chapter: Particulate Nature of Matter
questions.answer_explanation: Because particles are most closely packed in solids, they usually have the most mass per unit volume.

questions.question_text: A diver is able to cut through water in a swimming pool. Which property of matter does this observation show?
questions.subject: science
questions.chapter: Particulate Nature of Matter
questions.answer_explanation: The ability to move through a substance indicates that there are spaces between its particles and the forces of attraction can be overcome.

options.option_text: Particles of water are very large
There are spaces between particles of water

questions.question_text: Diffusion occurs fastest in gases because:
questions.subject: science
questions.chapter: Particulate Nature of Matter
questions.answer_explanation: Due to high speeds and large spaces between them, gas particles intermix with other particles very quickly.

options.option_text: Gas particles have low kinetic energy
Gas particles move rapidly in all directions

questions.question_text: Which of the following can be termed 'fluids'?
questions.subject: science
questions.chapter: Particulate Nature of Matter
questions.answer_explanation: Fluids are substances that can flow. Both liquids and gases have this ability.

questions.question_text: If you put a drop of ink in a glass of water, it spreads without stirring. This process is:
questions.subject: science
questions.chapter: Particulate Nature of Matter
questions.answer_explanation: Diffusion is the movement of particles from an area of higher concentration to lower concentration.

questions.question_text: In the 'Activity with the Syringe', why was it impossible to push the piston when it was filled with sand?
questions.subject: science
questions.chapter: Particulate Nature of Matter
questions.answer_explanation: Solids like sand have very little space between particles, making them nearly impossible to compress further.

options.option_text: Sand particles are already very close (solids are incompressible)

questions.question_text: Which of the following is the simplest form of matter that cannot be broken down into simpler substances by chemical means?
questions.subject: science
questions.chapter: Nature of Matter: Elements, Compounds, and Mixtures
questions.answer_explanation: An element is a pure substance made of only one kind of atom and cannot be split into simpler substances.

questions.question_text: When two or more elements combine chemically in a fixed ratio, they form a:
questions.subject: science
questions.chapter: Nature of Matter: Elements, Compounds, and Mixtures
questions.answer_explanation: A compound is a substance formed when atoms of two or more elements bond together chemically in a definite proportion by mass.

questions.question_text: Which of these is a characteristic of a mixture but NOT a compound?
questions.subject: science
questions.chapter: Nature of Matter: Elements, Compounds, and Mixtures
questions.answer_explanation: Mixtures are physically combined, so their components can be separated using physical means like filtration or evaporation.

options.option_text: Components are in a fixed ratio
It has a new set of properties
Components can be separated by physical methods

questions.question_text: The chemical formula for Common Salt is:
questions.subject: science
questions.chapter: Nature of Matter: Elements, Compounds, and Mixtures
questions.answer_explanation: Common salt is the compound Sodium Chloride, represented by the formula NaCl.

questions.question_text: Which of the following is an example of a homogeneous mixture?
questions.subject: science
questions.chapter: Nature of Matter: Elements, Compounds, and Mixtures
questions.answer_explanation: A homogeneous mixture has a uniform composition throughout; saltwater is a perfect example as the salt is evenly distributed.

questions.question_text: The symbol for the element Gold is:
questions.subject: science
questions.chapter: Nature of Matter: Elements, Compounds, and Mixtures
questions.answer_explanation: Au comes from the Latin word 'Aurum', which means gold.

questions.question_text: Water (H2O) is classified as a compound because:
questions.subject: science
questions.chapter: Nature of Matter: Elements, Compounds, and Mixtures
questions.answer_explanation: Compounds have a fixed chemical composition and the elements involved are bonded together.

options.option_text: It can be separated by filtration
Hydrogen and Oxygen are chemically bonded in a 2:1 ratio
It exists in three states: solid, liquid, and gas
It is a mixture of two gases

questions.question_text: Which of the following mixtures can be separated using a magnet?
questions.subject: science
questions.chapter: Nature of Matter: Elements, Compounds, and Mixtures
questions.answer_explanation: Iron is a magnetic material, so a magnet can easily pull iron filings out of a non-magnetic mixture like sand.

questions.question_text: Air is considered a mixture because:
questions.subject: science
questions.chapter: Nature of Matter: Elements, Compounds, and Mixtures
questions.answer_explanation: Air contains various gases like Nitrogen, Oxygen, and CO2 that are not chemically bonded and whose proportions can change.

options.option_text: Its composition of gases can vary slightly
It contains only one type of molecule

questions.question_text: When iron and sulfur are heated together to form iron sulfide, the product:
questions.subject: science
questions.chapter: Nature of Matter: Elements, Compounds, and Mixtures
questions.answer_explanation: Iron sulfide is a compound; once formed, it loses the individual properties of its constituent elements.

options.option_text: Retains the magnetic property of iron
Can be separated by a magnet
Has entirely different properties from iron and sulfur
Is a yellow and black mixture

questions.question_text: The horizontal rows in a Periodic Table are called:
questions.subject: science
questions.chapter: Nature of Matter: Elements, Compounds, and Mixtures
questions.answer_explanation: The Periodic Table is arranged in horizontal rows called periods and vertical columns called groups.

questions.question_text: Which of these is a noble gas?
questions.subject: science
questions.chapter: Nature of Matter: Elements, Compounds, and Mixtures
questions.answer_explanation: Helium is a noble gas (Group 18) because it is very stable and rarely reacts with other elements.

questions.question_text: A mixture of oil and water is an example of a:
questions.subject: science
questions.chapter: Nature of Matter: Elements, Compounds, and Mixtures
questions.answer_explanation: Oil and water do not mix uniformly and form visible separate layers, making it a heterogeneous mixture.

questions.question_text: What is the smallest unit of a compound that maintains its chemical properties?
questions.subject: science
questions.chapter: Nature of Matter: Elements, Compounds, and Mixtures
questions.answer_explanation: A molecule is the smallest particle of a compound that has the chemical properties of that compound.

questions.question_text: Brass is an alloy of Copper and Zinc. Brass is a:
questions.subject: science
questions.chapter: Nature of Matter: Elements, Compounds, and Mixtures
questions.answer_explanation: Alloys like brass are mixtures because the metals are physically combined and can vary in proportions.

questions.question_text: Which of the following is NOT matter?
questions.subject: science
questions.chapter: Nature of Matter: Elements, Compounds, and Mixtures
questions.answer_explanation: Matter must have mass and occupy space. Light is a form of energy and does not have mass.

questions.question_text: The elements in a compound are combined in a:
questions.subject: science
questions.chapter: Nature of Matter: Elements, Compounds, and Mixtures
questions.answer_explanation: According to the Law of Definite Proportions, a chemical compound always contains its component elements in fixed ratio.

questions.question_text: If you stir sand into water, you create a:
questions.subject: science
questions.chapter: Nature of Matter: Elements, Compounds, and Mixtures
questions.answer_explanation: Sand in water is a suspension, a type of heterogeneous mixture where particles settle down over time.

questions.question_text: Which gas is used in advertising signs for its bright glow when electricity passes through it?
questions.subject: science
questions.chapter: Nature of Matter: Elements, Compounds, and Mixtures
questions.answer_explanation: Neon is widely used in neon signs because it emits a bright reddish-orange glow.

questions.question_text: Pure gold is 24 carats. 22 carat gold is a:
questions.subject: science
questions.chapter: Nature of Matter: Elements, Compounds, and Mixtures
questions.answer_explanation: 22 carat gold contains other metals like copper or silver to make it harder, making it a mixture (alloy).

questions.question_text: Which scientist is famously associated with the development of the Periodic Table?
questions.subject: science
questions.chapter: Nature of Matter: Elements, Compounds, and Mixtures
questions.answer_explanation: Mendeleev is known as the father of the Periodic Table for organizing elements based on their properties.

questions.question_text: Which of these is a diatomic element (exists naturally as a molecule of two atoms)?
questions.subject: science
questions.chapter: Nature of Matter: Elements, Compounds, and Mixtures
questions.answer_explanation: Oxygen naturally exists as O2, meaning two oxygen atoms are bonded together.

questions.question_text: Distilled water is a/an:
questions.subject: science
questions.chapter: Nature of Matter: Elements, Compounds, and Mixtures
questions.answer_explanation: Distilled water consists only of H2O molecules, making it a pure compound.

questions.question_text: The process of separating a solid from a liquid by pouring it through a porous material is called:
questions.subject: science
questions.chapter: Nature of Matter: Elements, Compounds, and Mixtures
questions.answer_explanation: Filtration is a physical method used to separate insoluble solids from a liquid mixture.

questions.question_text: What happens when sugar dissolves in water?
questions.subject: science
questions.chapter: Nature of Matter: Elements, Compounds, and Mixtures
questions.answer_explanation: Sugar dissolving in water is a physical change that results in a homogeneous mixture (solution).

questions.question_text: Which of the following is an example of a uniform (homogeneous) mixture?
questions.subject: science
questions.chapter: The Amazing World of Solutes, Solvents, and Solutions
questions.answer_explanation: A uniform mixture is one where components are evenly distributed throughout. Salt dissolves completely in water to form such a mixture.

questions.question_text: In a solution of sugar and water, sugar is the __________ and water is the __________.
questions.subject: science
questions.chapter: The Amazing World of Solutes, Solvents, and Solutions
questions.answer_explanation: The substance being dissolved is the solute (sugar), and the substance that does the dissolving is the solvent (water).

questions.question_text: Water is often called a 'Universal Solvent' because:
questions.subject: science
questions.chapter: The Amazing World of Solutes, Solvents, and Solutions
questions.answer_explanation: Water has a unique ability to dissolve more substances than any other liquid, earning it the title of universal solvent.

options.option_text: It is found everywhere on Earth
It can dissolve a large variety of substances
It is used in all industrial processes

questions.question_text: A solution in which no more solute can be dissolved at a given temperature is called a/an:
questions.subject: science
questions.chapter: The Amazing World of Solutes, Solvents, and Solutions
questions.answer_explanation: When a solvent has dissolved the maximum amount of solute possible at a specific temperature, it becomes saturated.

questions.question_text: What happens to the solubility of sugar in water if the temperature of the water is increased?
questions.subject: science
questions.chapter: The Amazing World of Solutes, Solvents, and Solutions
questions.answer_explanation: Generally, increasing the temperature increases the solubility of solid solutes like sugar in a liquid solvent.

questions.question_text: Which of the following describes a 'dilute' solution?
questions.subject: science
questions.chapter: The Amazing World of Solutes, Solvents, and Solutions
questions.answer_explanation: A dilute solution contains a small amount of solute relative to the amount of solvent.

options.option_text: A solution with very little solute
A solution with a lot of solute
A solution that cannot dissolve more solute
A mixture of oil and water

questions.question_text: Which process is used to recover salt from sea water?
questions.subject: science
questions.chapter: The Amazing World of Solutes, Solvents, and Solutions
questions.answer_explanation: Evaporation allows the water to turn into vapor, leaving behind the solid salt crystals.

questions.question_text: Why do aquatic animals like fish survive in ponds and rivers?
questions.subject: science
questions.chapter: The Amazing World of Solutes, Solvents, and Solutions
questions.answer_explanation: Gases like oxygen dissolve in water, which aquatic animals use for respiration.

options.option_text: Oxygen from the air dissolves in the water
Water is made of oxygen atoms

questions.question_text: What is the result of mixing two or more substances that do not chemically react?
questions.subject: science
questions.chapter: The Amazing World of Solutes, Solvents, and Solutions
questions.answer_explanation: A mixture is formed when two or more substances are combined physically without a chemical reaction.

questions.question_text: Carbonated drinks (soda) are examples of:
questions.subject: science
questions.chapter: The Amazing World of Solutes, Solvents, and Solutions
questions.answer_explanation: Carbon dioxide gas is dissolved in the liquid drink under pressure.

questions.question_text: Which factor does NOT affect how fast a solid solute dissolves in a liquid?
questions.subject: science
questions.chapter: The Amazing World of Solutes, Solvents, and Solutions
questions.answer_explanation: While stirring, heat, and particle size (crushing) affect the rate of dissolution, the container's shape does not.

questions.question_text: If you have a saturated solution of salt, how can you make it unsaturated without adding more water?
questions.subject: science
questions.chapter: The Amazing World of Solutes, Solvents, and Solutions
questions.answer_explanation: Heating increases solubility, allowing the solution to hold more solute, thus making it temporarily unsaturated.

questions.question_text: Soft drinks fizz when opened because:
questions.subject: science
questions.chapter: The Amazing World of Solutes, Solvents, and Solutions
questions.answer_explanation: Gases are more soluble at high pressure. Opening the bottle drops the pressure, causing the dissolved CO2 to escape.

options.option_text: The pressure decreases, reducing gas solubility

questions.question_text: What is 'solubility'?
questions.subject: science
questions.chapter: The Amazing World of Solutes, Solvents, and Solutions
questions.answer_explanation: Solubility is the measure of how much solute can be held by a solvent under specific conditions.

options.option_text: The total volume of a solution
The speed at which a solute dissolves
The maximum amount of solute that can dissolve in a specific amount of solvent at a certain temperature
The ability of a liquid to flow

questions.question_text: Which of these is a solution of a gas in a gas?
questions.subject: science
questions.chapter: The Amazing World of Solutes, Solvents, and Solutions
questions.answer_explanation: Air is a homogeneous mixture (solution) of various gases like nitrogen, oxygen, and others.

questions.question_text: Small round cakes of salt in Ningel village are wrapped in:
questions.subject: science
questions.chapter: The Amazing World of Solutes, Solvents, and Solutions
questions.answer_explanation: As mentioned in the 'Living Heritage' section, traditional salt cakes are shaped using banana leaves.

questions.question_text: When sugar is dissolved in water, the volume of the water does not increase significantly because:
questions.subject: science
questions.chapter: The Amazing World of Solutes, Solvents, and Solutions
questions.answer_explanation: Liquid molecules have small spaces between them; dissolved solute particles occupy these spaces.

options.option_text: Sugar particles fit into the empty spaces between water molecules

questions.question_text: Which of the following will form a non-uniform (heterogeneous) mixture with water?
questions.subject: science
questions.chapter: The Amazing World of Solutes, Solvents, and Solutions
questions.answer_explanation: Oil and water do not mix and form distinct layers, creating a non-uniform mixture.

questions.question_text: How can you separate a mixture of sand and salt?
questions.subject: science
questions.chapter: The Amazing World of Solutes, Solvents, and Solutions
questions.answer_explanation: Salt is soluble in water but sand is not. Dissolving allows you to filter the sand and then recover salt by evaporation.

options.option_text: Dissolve in water, filter the sand, then evaporate the water

questions.question_text: The components of an Oral Rehydration Solution (ORS) are:
questions.subject: science
questions.chapter: The Amazing World of Solutes, Solvents, and Solutions
questions.answer_explanation: ORS is a uniform mixture of sugar and salt in water used to treat dehydration.

questions.question_text: Why is it easier to dissolve sugar in hot milk than in cold milk?
questions.subject: science
questions.chapter: The Amazing World of Solutes, Solvents, and Solutions
questions.answer_explanation: Heating the solvent increases the kinetic energy and solubility, making the solute dissolve faster and in greater amounts.

options.option_text: High temperature increases the rate of dissolving

questions.question_text: Which of the following is NOT a property of a true solution?
questions.subject: science
questions.chapter: The Amazing World of Solutes, Solvents, and Solutions
questions.answer_explanation: In a true solution, particles are so small they are invisible to the eye and do not settle.

options.option_text: The solute particles can be seen with a naked eye
The solute does not settle down over time
It can pass through a filter paper

questions.question_text: Tincture of iodine is a solution used as an antiseptic. What is the solvent in it?
questions.subject: science
questions.chapter: The Amazing World of Solutes, Solvents, and Solutions
questions.answer_explanation: Tincture of iodine is iodine (solute) dissolved in alcohol (solvent).

questions.question_text: What happens when you add more and more solute to a fixed amount of solvent?
questions.subject: science
questions.chapter: The Amazing World of Solutes, Solvents, and Solutions
questions.answer_explanation: Adding more solute increases its ratio to the solvent, making the solution more concentrated.

questions.question_text: A solution of two or more metals, or a metal and a non-metal, is called a/an:
questions.subject: science
questions.chapter: The Amazing World of Solutes, Solvents, and Solutions
questions.answer_explanation: Alloys are solid-in-solid solutions, such as brass (zinc in copper).
